title: organization — retention windows
description: Active + paused Organizations never expire. Archived rows hold for 730 days then hard-purge IF no descendants + no branch references. TenantErased cascades hard-delete via FK regardless of retention state.

purge.notes: Active + paused rows are always addressable. No automatic expiry — a tenant may keep hundreds of active orgs indefinitely (subject to entitlement caps).

purge.trigger: deleted_at + 730 days elapsed + no descendants + no branch references
purge.notes: The 730-day window is deliberately long — Organizations are often referenced by long-lived compliance reports (e.g. 'which brand ran which programme in 2024?'). Hard-purge blocks when descendants OR branches still reference — retention is HELD until those clear.

audit_retention.description: Audit rows (via `owen-it/laravel-auditing`) for Organization mutations follow the `audit::retention` tier-driven window (90d / 1y / 7y per plan). Retention DOES NOT cascade — an archived Organization's history is preserved for the full audit retention window even after the Organization itself has been hard-purged (rare but possible in the failure mode where audits outlive the row via async retention job races).

notes: The 730-day window is separate from the tier-driven audit retention. Rationale: the Organization row's absence signals 'this brand no longer exists' to the platform; the audit trail signals 'here is what happened before it went away' and must survive per compliance rules.
TenantErased always wins over the 730-day hold. GDPR Art. 17 obligations force cascade delete regardless of retention state.

Restore paths: an archived row within the 730-day window may be restored via `POST /organizations/{id}/restore` (permission organizations.restore.tenant — planned; observer already handles the restore lifecycle). After the 730-day window OR after hard-purge, restoration is impossible — a new row must be created with a new id.
